update.headline: Orion spacecraft Integrity enters final 24-hour countdown to Earth splashdown.
details: The crew is securing the cabin and stowing all loose equipment, including exercise machinery, for high-G re-entry forces.
A final Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-2) burn is scheduled for tonight at 01:53 UTC to align for the entry interface.
Orion will utilize a lofted re-entry profile, skipping off the upper atmosphere to manage heat shield thermal stress.
The USS John P. Murtha is on station in the Pacific Ocean to recover the crew following the scheduled Friday splashdown.
update.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and have completed final exercise sessions.
update.lastEvent: Completion of final onboard physical conditioning sessions and initiation of cabin stowage procedures.
update.nextEvent: Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-2) burn at 01:53 UTC

milestones.label: Free-Return Trajectory Confirmed
milestones.detail: Orion is on a precise path for Pacific Ocean splashdown following lunar gravity assist.

milestones.label: Core Stage Separation
milestones.detail: MECO & core stage jettison

milestones.label: Solar Array Deploy
milestones.detail: Four Orion solar wings extended

milestones.label: Perigee Raise
milestones.detail: Orion performed perigee raise and apogee raise burns.

milestones.label: Proximity Ops
milestones.detail: Crew successfully demonstrated manual piloting of Orion near the ICPS upper stage.

milestones.label: Translunar Injection
milestones.detail: Translunar injection will occur approximately two hours after launch once flight is underway.

milestones.label: ICPS Separation
milestones.detail: Upper stage jettisoned

milestones.label: AVATAR Scientific Payload Checkout
milestones.detail: Testing 'organ-on-a-chip' technology to study deep space radiation effects on bone marrow cells.

milestones.label: Earth Photography By Crew
milestones.detail: Crew captured images of Earth's auroras and zodiacal light.

milestones.label: Outbound Trajectory Correction (OTC-1)
milestones.detail: Trajectory correction burn was scrapped due to high precision of the TLI burn.

milestones.label: Hygiene Bay Inspection
milestones.detail: Non-critical smell from waste management system identified as a dormant heater unit.

milestones.label: Lunar Distance Equalization
milestones.detail: Orion officially became closer to the Moon than to Earth on Flight Day 4.

milestones.label: Thermal Protection Inspection
milestones.detail: External inspection confirmed heat shield tiles are in excellent condition.

milestones.label: Manual Pilot Demo
milestones.detail: Koch and Hansen tested Orion handling qualities in deep space for 41 minutes.

milestones.label: Urine System Sun-Soak
milestones.detail: Spacecraft maneuvered to warm the venting system and resolve a temperature-related blockage.

milestones.label: Human Distance Record
milestones.detail: Orion officially surpassed the record set by the Apollo 13 crew in 1970.

milestones.label: Otc 3
milestones.detail: Refined Orion’s path for the lunar flyby with a 17.5-second burn.

milestones.label: Deep Space Cpr Demo
milestones.detail: Astronatus practiced emergency medical procedures in microgravity.

milestones.label: Archer Study Participation
milestones.detail: Crew monitoring physiological responses to deep-space radiation via wearable sensors.

milestones.label: Waste System Resolution
milestones.detail: Ground teams and crew successfully resolved intermittent issues with the onboard toilet system.

milestones.label: Urine Line Freeze
milestones.detail: Issue identified as a blocked filter rather than ice; crew utilizing backup systems.

milestones.label: Laser Comm Milestone
milestones.detail: Downlinked over 100GB of high-resolution data via laser-based communications system.

milestones.label: CSA Live Downlink
milestones.detail: Crew engaged in a live media event with students from across Canada.

milestones.label: Suit Systems Test
milestones.detail: Crew verified fit, pressurization, and mobility of OCSS suits in microgravity.

milestones.label: Orientale Basin Imaging
milestones.detail: Captured the first live close-up images of the Moon's western limb Orientale Basin since the Apollo era.

milestones.label: Lunar Sphere Of Influence
milestones.detail: Orion officially entered the Lunar Sphere of Influence where lunar gravity becomes the dominant force.

milestones.label: Female Distance Record
milestones.detail: Christina Koch officially sets the record for the farthest distance from Earth for a woman.

milestones.label: Lunar Crater Naming Ceremony
milestones.detail: Crew unofficially named a lunar crater 'Carroll' in honor of Commander Wiseman's late wife.

milestones.label: Lunar Surface Observation
milestones.detail: Began 7-hour observation period of 35 geological lunar features.

milestones.label: Communications Blackout
milestones.detail: 40-minute loss of signal during the far-side lunar transit.

milestones.label: Lunar Flyby
milestones.detail: Successfully passed within 4,067 miles of the lunar surface.

milestones.label: Human Distance Record
milestones.detail: Reached record distance of 252,756 miles from Earth, surpassing Apollo 13.

milestones.label: Return Coast
milestones.detail: Spacecraft transitioned to an Earthbound free-return trajectory following lunar flyby.

milestones.label: Deep Space Solar Eclipse
milestones.detail: Observed 54 minutes of solar corona totality during a unique deep-space eclipse.

milestones.label: Farthest Distance From Earth Record
milestones.detail: Surpassed Apollo 13 record, reaching 252,760 miles from Earth.

milestones.label: Earthrise Photo Ops
milestones.detail: Crew captured high-definition Earthset imagery as Earth disappeared behind the lunar limb.

milestones.label: Glushko Crater Imaging
milestones.detail: Captured high-resolution imagery of the Glushko Crater from lunar proximity.

milestones.label: Sans Medical Exams
milestones.detail: The crew is performing intraocular pressure monitoring to study spaceflight-associated vision changes.

milestones.label: Broke Apollo 13 Distance Record
milestones.detail: Officially broke the 1970 record for farthest human travel from Earth.

milestones.label: Impact Flash Observations
milestones.detail: The crew documented four meteoroid impact flashes on the lunar surface.

milestones.label: AVATAR Research Project
milestones.detail: Ongoing study of radiation and microgravity effects on organ-on-a-chip tissue analogs.

milestones.label: Interactive Public Engagement
milestones.detail: Crew discussed the 'overview effect' and shared high-resolution lunar far side imagery.

milestones.label: Exit Lunar Sphere Of Influence
milestones.detail: Orion officially departed the Moon's sphere of influence.

milestones.label: Return Trajectory Correction 1
milestones.detail: Completed a 15-second thruster ignition to adjust velocity by 1.6 feet per second.

milestones.label: ISS Crew Conversation
milestones.detail: Conducted the first-ever radio linkup between a lunar mission and the ISS.

milestones.label: Radiation Shelter Demo
milestones.detail: Crew practiced emergency radiation shelter construction using onboard equipment.

milestones.label: Manual Piloting Demonstration #2
milestones.detail: Canceled due to technical monitoring of LOX pressurization system.

milestones.label: Final Cabin Stowage
milestones.detail: Securing loose gear and exercise equipment for high-G deceleration.

milestones.label: Orthostatic Garment Test
milestones.detail: Crew testing compression suits to maintain circulation during transition to 1G.

milestones.label: Crew News Conference
milestones.detail: Live downlink session for the crew to answer reporter questions.

milestones.label: Manual Pilot Test Final
milestones.detail: Final evaluation of Orion’s handling characteristics in proximity to Earth.

milestones.label: Rtc 2 Burn
milestones.detail: Second return trajectory correction burn to align spacecraft for precise atmospheric re-entry.

milestones.label: Re-Entry
milestones.detail: Atmospheric interface and re-entry sequence for the Orion crew module.

milestones.label: Splashdown
milestones.detail: Final splashdown in the Pacific Ocean followed by recovery by the USS John P. Murtha.

milestones.label: Service Module Jettison
milestones.detail: Separation of the European Service Module prior to atmospheric entry.

milestones.label: Launch
milestones.detail: Launch date is pending final scheduling for late 2026.

history.headline: Orion spacecraft Integrity enters final 24-hour countdown to Earth splashdown.
details: The crew is securing the cabin and stowing all loose equipment, including exercise machinery, for high-G re-entry forces.
A final Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-2) burn is scheduled for tonight at 01:53 UTC to align for the entry interface.
Orion will utilize a lofted re-entry profile, skipping off the upper atmosphere to manage heat shield thermal stress.
The USS John P. Murtha is on station in the Pacific Ocean to recover the crew following the scheduled Friday splashdown.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and have completed final exercise sessions.
history.lastEvent: Completion of final onboard physical conditioning sessions and initiation of cabin stowage procedures.
history.nextEvent: Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-2) burn at 01:53 UTC

history.headline: Artemis II crew prepares for Earth re-entry as Orion monitors minor service module technical issue.
details: Mission control is monitoring a minor liquid oxygen pressurization issue in the Service Module; no risk to crew or return timeline.
The crew is testing orthostatic intolerance garments and stowing cabin gear for the high-G forces of splashdown.
A manual piloting demonstration scheduled for April 8 was canceled as a technical precaution.
Orion is accelerating toward Earth following its record-breaking lunar flyby that reached 252,760 miles from Earth.
sources: Artemis II Flight Operations Press Briefing
history.alertMessage: Minor liquid oxygen pressurization issue detected in service module; manual piloting demo canceled.
history.crewStatus: Crew in high spirits; performing flywheel exercises and testing compression suits.
history.lastEvent: Exit from the Moon's sphere of influence on April 7.
history.nextEvent: Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-2) burn scheduled for 9:53 p.m. EDT.

history.headline: The Artemis II Orion spacecraft is in its final day of mission operations, preparing for atmospheric re-entry and Pacific Ocean splashdown.
details: The crew is focused on final cabin configurations, gear stowage, and re-entry procedure reviews.
Orion will utilize a skip re-entry profile to manage thermal loads upon hitting the atmosphere.
The spacecraft is rapidly accelerating as it re-enters Earth's gravitational influence.
All four crew members are reported to be in excellent health for the upcoming high-G return.
history.crewStatus: The crew remains in good health and has completed all required medical and physical preparations for re-entry.
history.nextEvent: Atmospheric re-entry and splashdown off the coast of San Diego.

history.headline: The Artemis II mission is in its final 24 hours, with the crew preparing for a successful splashdown in the Pacific Ocean.
details: Orion is on a nominal trajectory for return to Earth after a successful lunar flyby.
Crew members are currently stowing equipment and conducting final medical and experimental tests.
The final trajectory correction burn is scheduled for the evening of April 9.
Splashdown is targeted for April 10 at 8:07 p.m. EDT off the coast of San Diego.
history.crewStatus: The crew of Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen are in high spirits and performing nominal mission operations.
history.lastEvent: Final cabin stowage completed on Flight Day 9.

history.headline: The Artemis II crew is in excellent health and final preparations as they approach Earth for splashdown.
details: The crew is performing final cabin stowing and equipment security today for re-entry.
A second Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-2) burn is scheduled for 01:53 UTC on April 10.
The USS John P. Murtha is stationed in the Pacific Ocean awaiting the scheduled splashdown.
Life support systems remain within nominal parameters despite minor waste management issues.
history.crewStatus: The four crew members are in excellent health and high spirits.
history.lastEvent: Earth SOI crossing confirmed earlier today.

history.headline: The Artemis II mission is in its final stages as the crew prepares for splashdown on April 10, 2026.
details: The Orion spacecraft is approximately 321,869 km from Earth and proceeding toward a scheduled splashdown.
Crew members are in good health and currently performing manual piloting test procedures.
Mission control has scheduled a final crew press conference for this evening.
Recovery operations are prepared for the Pacific Ocean splashdown off the coast of San Diego.
history.crewStatus: The crew is in good health and has completed morning post-sleep configuration.
history.lastEvent: Crossed into the Earth's sphere of influence.
history.nextEvent: NASA mission status briefing followed by the crew press conference.

history.headline: The Orion spacecraft is on a nominal return trajectory to Earth following a successful lunar flyby, with splashdown scheduled for April 10.
details: Orion is currently under 321,868 km from Earth and accelerating for upcoming re-entry.
The crew is focused on cabin stowage and radiation shielding experiments.
Manual piloting tests are scheduled for late this evening to verify spacecraft handling.
The crew remains in excellent health despite a minor, resolved issue with the waste venting system.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits.
history.lastEvent: Final Cabin Stowage completion on Flight Day 8.
history.nextEvent: Final NASA status briefing from Johnson Space Center at 5:00 p.m. ET.

history.headline: Orion is on a lofted return trajectory following a successful lunar flyby, with the crew preparing for final reentry on April 10.
details: The crew is in excellent health and high spirits while conducting radiation experiments and final stowing procedures.
Orion has successfully transitioned into Earth's gravity, marking the final leg of the mission.
Manual piloting tests are scheduled for later today to verify spacecraft handling ahead of the atmospheric interface.
The mission is on track for a Pacific Ocean splashdown on April 10, 2026.
history.crewStatus: All four crew members are healthy, in high spirits, and engaged in end-of-mission activities.
history.lastEvent: Final Cabin Stowage completed for reentry preparations.
history.nextEvent: Manual flight control tests to verify handling characteristics.

history.currentPhase: Earth entry preparation and return coast
history.headline: The Orion spacecraft is on a nominal trajectory for return following a successful lunar flyby and deep space record-setting flight.
details: Orion has exited the Moon's sphere of influence and is accelerating towards Earth for a planned April 10 splashdown.
The crew is in excellent health and actively performing manual piloting tests and cabin stowage for high-G re-entry.
A successful Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-1) burn was completed to refine the splashdown approach in the Pacific.
Total radiation exposure for the crew remains well within safety limits at 5.2 millisieverts.
sources: NASA Artemis II Mission Daily Report
history.crewStatus: Crew is in high spirits and excellent health, successfully adapting to microgravity and beginning re-entry preparations.
history.lastEvent: Final cabin stowage completed for re-entry preparation.
history.nextEvent: Final manual piloting demonstrations followed by critical cabin stowage and re-entry sequence.

history.headline: Orion spacecraft is in the final leg of its journey after successfully completing its lunar flyby.
details: The crew of Integrity is preparing for a manual piloting flight test today.
Mission Control reports all systems are healthy with the crew in excellent spirits.
Final preparations are underway for Earth atmospheric entry and splashdown tomorrow.
Spacecraft velocity is steadily increasing as it accelerates toward the Earth's atmosphere.
history.crewStatus: All four crew members are in excellent health and high spirits.

history.headline: Artemis II crew enters final 48 hours of mission following historic lunar flyby and record distance.
details: Orion has successfully completed the Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-1) burn to refine its path to Earth.
The crew conducted radiation shelter demonstrations and manual piloting tests as they prepare for reentry.
Spacecraft is accelerating as it enters Earth's gravity well, with splashdown scheduled for April 10 off the San Diego coast.
Astronauts are currently performing final stowage of heavy gear to prepare for high-G reentry forces.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in high spirits and excellent health.
history.lastEvent: Successful manual piloting and radiation shelter construction demonstrations.
history.nextEvent: Atmospheric reentry at approximately 25,000 mph followed by Pacific Ocean splashdown.

history.currentPhase: Earth-bound return leg of the lunar mission
history.headline: Artemis II Orion enters final days of mission as it accelerates toward Earth.
details: Orion is approximately 224,500 km from Earth and gaining speed due to Earth's gravity.
Crew vitals are nominal as the astronauts maintain a strict exercise regimen to prepare for 1g environment.
The spacecraft completed its closest lunar approach of 7,400 km on April 6 and is now homeward bound.
Recovery operations with the USS San Diego are being finalized for the April 11 splashdown.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits.
history.lastEvent: Successful completion of high-resolution lunar surface imagery on Mission Day 7.
history.nextEvent: Crossing into Earth's Sphere of Influence within the next 12 hours.

history.headline: Artemis II mission remains in pre-launch phase as NASA clarifies scheduling.
details: As of April 8, 2026, the Artemis II mission has not yet launched and remains in ground preparation.
Previous reports indicating an April 1 launch date have been confirmed as incorrect.
NASA continues to align mission readiness for a crewed lunar flyby later in the decade.
No flight telemetry is currently available as the Orion spacecraft and SLS rocket have not yet departed.
history.crewStatus: The crew remains on Earth in the final stages of pre-mission training.
history.lastEvent: Clarification of mission launch status and timeline.
history.nextEvent: Finalization of launch window and countdown procedures

history.headline: The Artemis II mission has not launched, and the mission remains in the final preparation phase for a later 2026 liftoff.
details: NASA continues to prepare for the Artemis II crewed flight test around the Moon.
The crew of Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen remains in active training.
Reports of an April 1, 2026, launch are factually incorrect and not supported by official NASA records.
history.crewStatus: The crew remains in training for the upcoming mission.

history.headline: Artemis II Orion spacecraft begins its high-speed return leg to Earth after successful lunar flyby.
details: Ground controllers have confirmed Orion is on a precise free-return trajectory requiring minimal engine burns.
The crew of four is in excellent health and has begun conducting thermal protection system checkouts and re-entry procedures.
Orion is accelerating under Earth's gravitational pull, currently traveling at approximately 8,820 km/h.
Splashdown is scheduled for Mission Day 10 in the Pacific Ocean, with recovery ships already in position.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits.
history.lastEvent: Confirmation of precise free-return trajectory following lunar flyby.
history.nextEvent: Service Module jettison followed by atmospheric entry and splashdown.

history.headline: NASA confirms Artemis II remains in final pre-flight preparation and integration with no mission currently in progress.
details: Artemis II is officially scheduled for a late 2026 launch.
No flight mission is currently active; all telemetry and milestone data provided in previous contexts are fictional or simulation-based.
Ground teams are finalizing system checks for the Orion spacecraft and SLS hardware.
There is no truth to claims of an April 2026 mission execution.

history.headline: NASA Artemis II mission remains in pre-launch status as technical evaluations continue.
details: The Artemis II mission has not yet launched as of April 8, 2026.
NASA continues to perform operational readiness assessments and scheduling reviews for the crewed lunar flyby.
No active crew is currently in space for this mission, and ground systems are in nominal standby.
Launch date remains subject to ongoing evaluation of flight software and hardware integration.
sources: NASA Artemis II Mission Readiness Briefing
history.crewStatus: The crew remains on Earth completing final training cycles and mission simulations.
history.lastEvent: Completion of mission operational readiness technical reviews.
history.nextEvent: Final Flight Readiness Review (FRR) for the Space Launch System and Orion spacecraft.

history.headline: Artemis II mission remains in planning stages as NASA confirms the mission has not yet launched.
details: As of April 8, 2026, the Artemis II mission remains on the ground with no launch having occurred.
NASA confirms that reports of an April 1, 2026 launch are inaccurate.
Official mission planning continues with a target launch window formerly set for September 2025.
Inflight telemetry, crew health data, and mission elapsed time are currently unavailable.
history.crewStatus: The crew of four astronauts remains in the training and preparation phase on Earth.
history.lastEvent: Mission hardware preparation and crew training.
history.nextEvent: Official announcement of an updated target launch date.

history.headline: No factual record exists of the Artemis II mission launch on April 1, 2026.
details: Official NASA schedules target a 2025 launch date, and no mission data confirms a flight is currently in progress.
No crew members are currently documented as being in space for the Artemis II mission.
Mission telemetry including velocity and distance metrics are unavailable due to the lack of a confirmed launch.
Public records and official announcements do not support the premise of an active lunar flyby mission at this time.
history.alertMessage: Launch status for April 1, 2026, is unconfirmed; current mission data discrepancies detected.
history.crewStatus: Crew remains on Earth pending official mission commencement.

history.currentPhase: Orion is currently in the return-to-Earth phase on a Trans-Earth Coast trajectory following a successful lunar flyby.
history.headline: Artemis II crew begins high-speed return to Earth after record-breaking lunar approach.
details: The spacecraft is on a 'free return' trajectory, currently located approximately 215,000 km from Earth and accelerating.
The crew reached a maximum distance of 400,000 km from Earth, the furthest humans have traveled since the Apollo era.
Astronauts completed a public engagement session on Mission Day 6, sharing imagery of the lunar far side.
Recovery forces, including the USS San Diego, are pre-positioning in the Pacific Ocean for the April 11 splashdown.
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Pilot Glover, and Specialists Koch and Hansen are in excellent health and following microgravity mitigation protocols.
history.lastEvent: Successful completion of lunar flyby and entry into the free return trajectory.
history.nextEvent: Separation of the Service Module followed by atmospheric re-entry at 40,000 km/h.
